event: Early humans inhabit the Nile Valley region of present-day Sudan, leaving Middle Stone Age tools.
Hunter-gatherer and fishing communities settle along the Nile, developing some of Africa's earliest pottery.
Neolithic cattle-herding cultures flourish in the eastern Sahara and Nubia.
The Kingdom of Kerma rises in Nubia, becoming one of Africa's earliest urban civilizations and a rival of Egypt.
Egypt's New Kingdom conquers and absorbs Nubia, ending the Kerma state.
The Kingdom of Kush emerges at Napata, asserting independence from Egypt.
Kushite king Piye conquers Egypt, founding the 25th Dynasty of Black Pharaohs.
Kush relocates its capital to Meroe, beginning the flourishing Meroitic civilization known for iron-working and pyramids.
The Kingdom of Aksum sacks Meroe, hastening the decline of the Kushite kingdom.
Christian missionaries convert the Nubian kingdoms of Nobatia, Makuria, and Alodia.
Makuria repels Arab invaders and signs the Baqt treaty, a centuries-long peace and trade pact with Muslim Egypt.
Makuria's cathedral at Dongola is converted into a mosque as Islam spreads into Nubia.
The Funj Sultanate of Sennar is established, uniting much of central Sudan under a Muslim dynasty.
The Keira Sultanate consolidates power in Darfur as a major Saharan trade and slaving state.
Muhammad Ali of Egypt invades and conquers Sudan, founding Khartoum as the administrative capital.
Muhammad Ahmad declares himself the Mahdi and launches a religious revolt against Egyptian-Ottoman rule.
Mahdist forces capture Khartoum and kill General Charles Gordon, establishing an independent Mahdist state.
Anglo-Egyptian forces crush the Mahdist army at the Battle of Omdurman, ending the Mahdist state.
The Anglo-Egyptian Condominium is established, placing Sudan under joint British and Egyptian rule.
Britain annexes the Darfur Sultanate, killing Sultan Ali Dinar and incorporating it into Sudan.
Nationalist officers and the White Flag League stage uprisings against British colonial rule.
Britain and Egypt agree to grant Sudan self-government as a step toward independence.
Southern soldiers mutiny at Torit, igniting the First Sudanese Civil War.
Sudan gains full independence on 1 January and joins the United Nations.
General Ibrahim Abboud seizes power in Sudan's first military coup.
Colonel Jaafar Nimeiri takes power in a coup, establishing a long authoritarian rule.
The Addis Ababa Agreement ends the First Civil War and grants the south regional autonomy.
Nimeiri imposes Islamic sharia law and revokes southern autonomy, triggering the Second Sudanese Civil War.
A popular uprising and military coup overthrow President Nimeiri.
Omar al-Bashir seizes power in an Islamist-backed coup, dissolving parliament and banning political parties.
The United States designates Sudan a state sponsor of terrorism.
The U.S. bombs the Al-Shifa pharmaceutical factory in Khartoum, alleging links to chemical weapons.
Conflict erupts in Darfur as rebels rise up and government-backed Janjaweed militias commit mass atrocities.
The Comprehensive Peace Agreement ends the Second Civil War and grants southern Sudan autonomy.
The International Criminal Court issues an arrest warrant for President al-Bashir over Darfur war crimes.
South Sudan secedes after a referendum, becoming an independent nation and separating from Sudan.
Mass protests lead the military to oust President al-Bashir after three decades in power.
A power-sharing Sovereignty Council is formed between the military and civilian protest leaders.
The transitional government signs the Juba Peace Agreement with several rebel groups.
General Abdel Fattah al-Burhan leads a military coup, dissolving the civilian transitional government.
War erupts between the army and the Rapid Support Forces, devastating Khartoum and displacing millions.
